summary: Drew DeVault writes an open letter to Senator Bob Casey opposing the EARN IT Act, arguing it amounts to a ban on end-to-end encryption disguised as child protection legislation. He explains that the mathematics of strong encryption are unbreakable and publicly known, making any ban futile. He argues that the same encryption protecting criminals also protects journalists, activists, and national security. He concludes that good police work, not weakened encryption, is the proper tool for catching criminals, and that the right to private communication is fundamental to democracy.
key_points: The math behind strong encryption is proven unbreakable and fits on a single sheet of paper — banning it is as futile as banning algebra
Tor was designed by the US Navy and DARPA; weakening encryption for law enforcement undermines national security
Criminals will continue using encryption regardless of bans, so law enforcement must develop investigative capabilities that work within constitutional limits
The EARN IT Act uses the morally compelling issue of child abuse as a front for attacking fundamental privacy rights
End-to-end encryption is essential infrastructure for journalists, activists, watchdog groups, and democratic society
money_quotes: Asking service providers to solve them or stop using it is akin to forcing us to solve time travel or quit recording history.
The technology necessary to implement unbreakable encryption can be described succinctly on a single 8.5"x11" sheet of paper.
Attempting to ban it is equivalent to trying to ban algebra or trigonometry.
Breaking end-to-end encryption is no substitute for good police work and effective courts.

It's often difficult to conduct an investigation within the limits of the law and with respect to the rights of our citizens, but it's necessary for law enforcement to endure this difficulty to protect our freedom.
The EARN IT Act appears to use important and morally compelling problems of child abuse as a front for an attack on end-to-end encryption.
As freedom of the press is an early signal for the failure of democracy and rise of tyranny, so holds for the right to encrypt.
I am free to solve a simple mathematical equation which guarantees that my thoughts are shared only with those I choose.
key_insight: Banning end-to-end encryption is mathematically futile, strategically counterproductive to national security, and morally reprehensible when disguised as child protection.
